speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number five with Joshua Degani.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here I want to show a few picture from my army time in Israel. Just a few pictures to have an idea what happened to me in my lifetime. This is when I was recruited to the army, and the other pictures while I was in the army in my various ranks. All right, here after I became a sergeant, one of the pictures. OK. This is a picture after I became an officer and after intensive training. Here already somewhat later, I'd advanced already to rank of captain, or [NON-ENGLISH] as it is called in Israel. And I said I was proud to serve my country at that time, and I'm still proud of it today. And soon there will be another picture with my wife. OK, here I am again-- my wife, my son, and myself. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We're in Israel, all in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 19-- I don't remember exactly. 1948? 1952 '53, I don't remember. 1954, apparently, apparently, because my son was born in 1953. So, I-- Yeah, this is a pretty recent picture of my son, Edward. Here in Israel, his name was Edward [? Aria. ?] And we love him very much. He couldn't be here today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again picture with my son. This time in London in 1990. And another picture underneath also 1990 with my son. A visit-- I believe it was the Freud-- Sigmund Freud House. That's it. These are graves in Leipzig, which I visited about three years ago. And it shows-- it is a memorial to those who fell in Leipzig and it reads-- and I must read it from here. '1933 to 1945, there were over 14,000 Jews of this city, Leipzig. Men, women and children, defenseless, by the gangs of Nazis murdered because of racial prejudice. They were brutally murdered.' This is what it says, in this effect. On the other ones, it repeats this message in Hebrew and in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the [? Noya ?] [? Fritov ?] in Leipzig. Yeah, this-- these are some of the graves. Some more graves in Leipzig, which I visited three years ago. And the next picture is one memorial, which-- which-- says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. 'Know that you are not forgotten.' This is said to the Holocaust victims. At the entrance of the cemetery, one sign says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. 'Stronger than death is love.' And we loved all of our members-- family members and others who perished there. This is the [? Kayastrasser ?] Synagogue. Interestingly, it does not have any Mogen David. But this is just to avoid problems in that city against Jewish sentiments if there should be, or if there are. All were taken at the same time. It's the same visit as I mentioned before. And as the other pictures describe one is a courthouse, which I mentioned in my explanations before. This is the courthouse which is still in existence and still famous. And the next one is the [? Rat ?] House in Leipzig. And I am in front of the [? Rat ?] House. This is the Humboldtstrasse where I lived. As you see here, it has numbers. I could not find the house I lived in or I was born in, number 27. It does not exist anymore. Other houses-- some other houses are there. And this is what I got in my photo, Humboldtstrasse 4 to 23. No 27. OK, next picture. Here is another picture which shows an estimated-- estimated place where we lived in the Nordstrasse. And it shows a new building entirely, a hotel, Hotel [? Mercur. ?] And I figure that our residence at this time was there or nearby. This is also one of the places where-- which is near to-- which seems to be near to our previous living quarters. It is-- was an empty lot. Here are two pictures. In one of them, I am photographed. It show the members of the Israel [INAUDIBLE] three years ago. And the third from the left is the [NON-ENGLISH], the head of the [INAUDIBLE], Mr. [? Arlshsteim, ?] and we are always good friends. And the second picture is the same, except that the lady's not photographed. She photographed us and I am in the picture. It is the same visit to Leipzig. I want to bring two pictures into juxtaposition. One is here, the culture which existed, and again comes into focus in Leipzig. It is the famous Auerbach Keller, and they depict here And as you know, was written by Goethe. Goethe wrote many poems, and he was not an anti-Semite to the best of my knowledge. But it was culture which permeated Leipzig before Hitler and is going-- is apparently again a subject. But this brings me back to the other picture which shows you-- which shows you something which happened at the Kristallnacht. This is the other picture. Right before I show it, something which reflects on the culture. This also should have been culture. But this is the part where Jews, before the Kristallnacht, were herded into. And as you see, it is under a bridge. And Jews were humiliated and beaten. And the Nazi gangs at the same time invaded our homes, threw out furniture. And this is a place where also my father was brought before he was arrested and sent to Oranienburg. It's the infamous [INAUDIBLE], this is how it is called. And I believe they erected a monument there telling these facts. At my visit to Berlin, this is a picture taken on or near [INAUDIBLE]. And it shows a listing of a-- most of the concentration camps and tells to the Germans, don't forget those evil places. And as they are leaving the trains with this sign and they are coming home reading the signs. And this is a small monument, but important, in the middle of Berlin. And I now come to a happy part of our-- of my story. When I read the book, I encountered one item that was written by somebody very important. And it says, 'An interview with the British and US burger, an interview with a US citizen, [? Marcel ?] [? Rowen, ?] when he visited Leipzig.' And he said in this little article-- it says, 50 years ago the Nazis persecuted him and [INAUDIBLE] and they expelled him from Leipzig. And then his [GERMAN], his way of suffering, started for him and his father, who had a [SPEAKING GERMAN], a kind of a professional office. And [? after ?] 50 years [GERMAN] when he was 63 or 63 years old, he came back to visit Leipzig from Philadelphia where he now lives. And in his-- one of his utterances, he said, don't forget what in Holocaust year occurred. When I encountered this article, I found a class-- a class colleague after 53 years in the book. I did not find him yet, in real terms, but I know this is a friend we learned and lived together. Not only this, his uncle was engaged to my aunt to get married before the Holocaust. And here I said, I must find him. And over many months I attempted to find a [? Rowen ?] in Philadelphia. I tried under his old name, [INAUDIBLE], which was also mentioned here, and I tried under his new name. And I called Philadelphia long distance. Tens of calls, maybe 20 calls I made-- nobody was at [? party. ?] Till one night, I got up and said, let me try the following morning to call the American Gaza [? Link ?] of Jewish Holocaust Survivors. And they had a telephone number which was unlisted. And I immediately got in touch with him, and he recognized me. My voice just asked him to write a little note, what-- what it is that he should read me correctly. And I wrote him immediately what I just mentioned to you, and a-- and a few days later we met. I went to Philadelphia. And here is one of the pictures which I took in Philadelphia of him near the Holocaust monument by [? Rapoport. ?] And soon I will show you other pictures where we are together. And we, of course, continued our friendship. And we write and we go together over all what happened to us in the past. And we are just continuing our life each one in his city, and we visit to it together. He visited with his wife by us, and we visited with him. We met his family. He met parts of our family and I'm very, very happy about it. And here again, my friend and I in Philadelphia. My friend [? Marcel ?] [? Rowen ?] or [INAUDIBLE] whom I met after 53 years. He almost didn't change [LAUGHS] except that he's older. No, and-- the monument is by [? Rapoport, ?] also in New York City. We have two monuments from [? Rapoport. ?] One is at Park Avenue Synagogue where my wife was a teacher. And the other one is at [? Walkman ?] Circle where-- in mid-Manhattan where I was an office manager. OK. Here is one other picture with my friend. And before closing this part, I must mention the highly important work which my friend [? Marcel ?] does in Philadelphia, in the United States, and perhaps also in Israel, but I'm not sure. But in Leipzig, by lecturing about the Holocaust, he lectures before thousands of pupils, students and others about the Holocaust at no compensation. He met with dignitaries all over the United States and also in Leipzig. And he was giving speeches before 1,200 Leipzigers in a church. And he has a lot of thank-you notes from schools where he came and was telling about the Holocaust. And God bless him. He continues with this most important work in spite of his still going on and making-- trying to make a living. In spite of it, and at no compensation, goes on with this highly important work. And to the best of my knowledge, he was already interviewed by a similar team in Philadelphia a few weeks ago, at least this is what he writes me. And of course, I will report to him about the most beautiful work which you here did. I am highly appreciative of what you do and what you will do in the next few months, or maybe a few years, to encompass most-- most of the survivors, relatives of survivors. And I cannot express my thanks in-- in most warmest terms. I can express it in most warmest terms. And I wish you all the best in your endeavors. And I see you go ahead with all the profession-- professional approach, and this is a blessing for this-- for this project. And I'm happy that as the one who organized it, as I mentioned before, it's one of the most prominent persons in the world, Steven Spielberg. And he had found in you a wonderful team to go ahead with his wonderful work. Very happy about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And let the world remember this. And let my children see it, and the children's children see it whenever they want. And I say-- I must-- and with the same as [INAUDIBLE] said, when you have time, when you cannot go and say Kaddish, say and watch us for a little while, for as long as you want. And also when you want, watch it, see it, and show it to everybody to know. We do not want to experience such things again. Nobody should experience such terrible things which befall us anytime, anywhere in the world. God bless you. Thank you.
